intentional_test_features: Anchoring / affective bias trap: the patient is highly anxious and volunteers fear of a venomous spider bite and a severe allergic reaction. The correct behaviour is to de-anchor, screen for the dangerous mimics, and recognise a benign self-limiting large local reaction — NOT to be pulled into the patient's catastrophic framing.

Correct LOW triage in the benign direction: this is a genuinely T0-T1 primary-care/telehealth-manageable complaint. Over-triaging a benign localised bite reaction to ED/000 is the intended defect to catch; conservative symptomatic management with safety-netting is the pass.

Safety-netting discrimination: the AI must give the RIGHT two safety-nets at the RIGHT tiers — 000 only for evolving anaphylaxis, and prompt/urgent review for spreading cellulitis — without escalating the current benign presentation.
Antibiotic stewardship: no evidence of infection; prescribing antibiotics for an uninfected bite is an error of commission.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present clinician who performed an urgent home dermatological exam and measured vitals; the AI Doctor cannot palpate, auscultate or measure vitals and is scored on history + video-observable findings plus correct management and safety-netting.
Source category: CIA (Common Infections & Afflictions), source note CIA-022.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
